text: Hi, my name is Dr. Robert Richard, and what you're about to see is a procedure video of a vertical sleeve gastrectomy with hiatal hernia repair, which is a procedure that's commonly coupled with the vertical sleeve gastrectomy.
This is a relatively unedited video, and if there's any questions, you can certainly contact us at our office or go to our website at thelongstreetclinic.com.
So this is a laparoscopic vertical sleeve gastrectomy.
We're beginning the procedure.
The surgeon retracts the left lobe of the liver and begins to separate the gastroesophageal fat pad from the stomach using graspers.
The assistant approaches from the patient's right side as the surgeon uses a ligature device, identifiable by its white tip, to perform the procedure.
The surgeon uses a ligature device to cauterize and cut through blood vessels, visibly separating them during the hernia repair procedure.
The surgeon uses a cauterizing device to gently separate and cauterize the blood vessels attaching the gastroesophageal fat pad to the stomach, facilitating the removal of the fat pad.
The surgeon removes the gastroesophageal fat pad to expose the apex of the stomach, ensuring the fundus is not left large.
The surgeon identifies a hiatal hernia, with visible left and right crura of the diaphragm and a space between them.
So we're probably going to have to address this hiatal hernia at some point during this procedure.
The surgeon continues to dissect and remove the fat pad by cauterizing and cutting through the blood vessels attaching it to the stomach.
The surgeon completely removes the fat pad to achieve adequate exposure of the angle of His, the apex of the stomach where it angles into the esophagus.
The surgeon completely separates and removes the fat pad, providing clear exposure of the angle of His at the apex of the stomach.
The surgeon confirms complete access to the left crux of the diaphragm and the angle between the stomach and esophagus, with visible anatomical structures exposed for further surgical intervention.
The surgeon begins dissecting along the greater curvature of the stomach, removing surrounding tissue to enhance exposure for the hernia repair procedure.
The surgeon palpates the pylorus to identify its location, visible as a distinct anatomical structure at the distal end of the stomach.
The surgeon identifies an area between four and six centimeters proximal to the pylorus along the greater curve of the stomach, visually marking this section of the stomach's curvature.
And once we've identified an area where we're going to begin,
The surgeon begins cauterizing the blood vessels that attach the stomach to the colon and spleen, using an electrocautery device to separate these structures.
The surgeon uses a vessel sealing device, known as the ligature device, to cauterize tissue and enter the lesser sac, the space located behind the stomach.
The surgeon enters the lesser sac, a space located behind the stomach, using a vessel sealing device.
The surgeon rapidly cauterizes through large blood vessels with the assistance of an assistant who splays the vessels for individual cauterization, ensuring effective control of bleeding.
Once in this space, this becomes a very rapid procedure.
The surgeon cauterizes through the short gastric vessels, which are visibly close to the spleen, using a vessel sealing device.
The surgeon cauterizes friable blood vessels that tend to bleed and retract into the spleen, using a vessel sealing device to control bleeding effectively.
So we tend to slow down once we get close to the spleen.
The surgeon carefully dissects and cauterizes individual blood vessels near the spleen, ensuring precise control to prevent bleeding.
because if they retract into the spleen, they're very hard to control bleeding.
So this is one of the parts of the procedure where you'll notice that we'll probably noticeably slow down as we're coming through these vessels that we know can cause dangerous bleeding.
Because of this, one of the potential complications of a sleeve gastrectomy is bleeding as well as splenic injury and possibly the need for splenic removal.
The surgeon carefully dissects through the vessels located at the apex of the stomach, proceeding with gentle manipulation to avoid bleeding.
The surgeon exposes the posterior aspect of the stomach and the left cruce of the diaphragm after passing the vessels entering the spleen.

The surgeon has removed the anterior fat pad, providing clear exposure of the diaphragm in front of the stomach.
The surgeon ensures clear exposure of the posterior aspect of the stomach and the left cruce of the diaphragm, adjusting the surgical field for optimal visibility.
The surgeon continues to expose the area around the backside of the stomach and the left cruce of the diaphragm, ensuring clear visibility of these anatomical structures.
The surgeon achieves adequate exposure of the diaphragm, fully revealing the fundus of the stomach, ensuring no large posterior fundus is left unexposed.
The surgeon observes a significant hiatal hernia in the patient, visible as a protrusion at the diaphragm area.
The surgeon opens the space both anteriorly and posteriorly to the stomach, enhancing exposure of the surgical field around the diaphragm and stomach.
The surgeon begins the dissection of the hiatal hernia, focusing on separating the hernia sac from surrounding tissues near the diaphragm and stomach.
The surgeon enters the space near the diaphragm, continuing the dissection around the hiatal hernia.
The surgeon separates the diaphragm from the esophagus, carefully dissecting the tissue to delineate the anatomical structures involved in the hiatal hernia repair.
The surgeon carefully dissects through the small hernia sac, revealing the contents and surrounding structures within the hiatal region.
The assistant gently separates and exposes the tissue, allowing the surgeon to open up the hiatal hernia space, revealing the anatomical structures involved in the hernia repair.
The surgeon exposes the left cruciate diaphragm and begins to expose the right cruciate diaphragm by dissecting through the hernia sac, revealing the distal esophagus.
So at this point we're pretty happy with the exposure there.
The surgeon has mobilized the greater curvature of the stomach, preparing to initiate the sleeve gastrectomy procedure.
A 34 French bougie is passed down the esophagus, visible as it descends through the hiatal hernia space.
The surgeon passes a 34 French bougie into the lower part of the stomach, known as the antrum, as part of the hernia repair procedure.
The surgeon uses a stapling device to create the sleeve gastrectomy, placing three rows of staples on each side while cutting down the center of the stomach.
The surgeon uses a tri-stapler device, which deploys three different sizes of staples to create an adequate seal and control bleeding during the sleeve gastrectomy procedure.
The bougie is positioned within the stomach, and the surgeon prepares to place the first staple line along the mobilized greater curvature of the stomach.

The surgeon begins the staple line for the vertical sleeve gastrectomy, with the assistant holding the stomach in a natural position. The stapling device is used to place staples vertically alongside the bougie, which serves as a guide for the procedure.
The surgeon staples across the apex of the stomach, leaving a small portion of stomach tissue at the apex to avoid stapling across the esophagus.
The surgeon ensures that the complete staple line is positioned accurately along the gastric wall, verifying the alignment of the staples with the stomach tissue.
The surgeon begins to over-sew the staple line on the gastric wall, ensuring reinforcement and sealing of the stapled area.
The surgeon begins at the top of the surgical field, placing a simple Vicryl suture, an absorbable suture, to secure the tissue.
You can find it in any operating room.
The surgeon begins to over-sew the staple line on the gastric wall using a running, inverting suture technique, progressing from the top to the bottom of the staple line.
The surgeon completes the suture line, fully over-sewing the vertical sleeve gastrectomy staple line using a running, inverting technique.

have to deal with this hiatal hernia.
The surgeon exposes the esophagus along with the left and right crura of the diaphragm, revealing the apex of the sleeve gastrectomy.
The surgeon prepares to repair a large hiatal hernia using an endostitch suturing device, which facilitates the passage of sutures while minimizing tissue injury.
The surgeon uses an endostitch device to pass a permanent suture, completing the repair of the hiatal hernia.
The surgeon uses an endostitch device to pass a suture through both crura of the diaphragm, demonstrating rapid and controlled movement.
The surgeon closes the posterior part of the diaphragm using sutures, addressing the hiatal hernia repair.
The surgeon closes the hiatal hernia using the endostitch device, then assesses the anterior space to ensure complete closure.
And if we do, we will probably place a suture in the anterior diaphragm as well.
The surgeon performs a posterior diaphragmatic repair on the patient's hiatal hernia, focusing on the posterior aspect of the diaphragm.
The surgeon identifies a small space anteriorly after completing the posterior repair and places a figure-of-eight permanent suture to close the diaphragmatic defect both anteriorly and posteriorly.
The surgeon uses a suturing device to rapidly and accurately place a suture in the diaphragm, enhancing the precision of the repair.
The surgeon visually assesses the repaired diaphragmatic area, confirming the adequacy of the closure both anteriorly and posteriorly.
The surgeon removes the esophageal bougie, which was used to create a narrow sleeve during the hernia repair procedure.
After removing the esophageal bougie, the surgeon tacks the apex of the stomach, or the dog ear, to the diaphragm to prevent sleeve herniation into the mediastinum or chest.
The surgeon completes the creation of a narrow sleeve gastrectomy, with the stomach visibly shaped into a narrow sleeve configuration.
The surgeon completes the hiatal hernia repair by tacking the apex of the stomach to the left crux of the diaphragm, securing the anatomical structures in place.
The surgeon performs an upper endoscopy to visually inspect the repaired hiatal hernia and sleeve gastrectomy, ensuring the integrity of the surgical repair.
The assistant covers the surgical area with saline, while the surgeon passes a flexible Olympus endoscope down the esophagus.
The surgeon inspects the lower part of the stomach, ensuring an adequate lumen is maintained, checking for air leaks, and confirming there is no internal bleeding.
The surgeon observes the distended sleeve gastrectomy, confirming visually that there is no air leakage from the structure.
The external view shows the flexible Olympus endoscope positioned within the esophagus, providing visualization of the scope from outside the body.
The surgeon removes air from the stomach, and the flexible Olympus endoscope is withdrawn from the esophagus.
The surgeon prepares to placate the distended sleeve in the patient's stomach to prevent twisting, ensuring the sleeve remains stable.
The surgeon places one or two placating sutures between the omental fat and the lateral aspect of the gastric sleeve to prevent twisting and potential obstructions.
The surgeon removes the suture material from the previously placated sleeve, ensuring no twisting or obstruction occurs.
The surgeon observes the sleeve, noting that it is laying straight without any visible signs of twisting or obstruction.
The surgeon applies a fibrin sealant over the surgical site as a final step to prevent leaks.
Whether this helps or not is kind of debatable, but I've been using it for quite some time now.
The surgeon applies fibrin sealant along the suture line of the gastric sleeve, starting at the apex and progressing down the entire length of the suture line to ensure a secure seal.
The surgeon applies a final layer of fibrin sealant along the suture line of the gastric sleeve, ensuring a complete seal to prevent leaks.
The surgeon removes the liver retractor, revealing the surgical field with the gastric sleeve laying straight and untwisted.
The surgeon locates the stomach specimen within the surgical field, preparing for further steps in the procedure.
The surgeon grasps the apex of the stomach, the narrow part, and removes it through a laparoscopic incision, marking the conclusion of the procedure.
